---
name: mempal-memory
description: Search and record durable engineering knowledge with the mempal CLI, before and after coding work.
---

# Mempal memory protocol for this eval

This environment has a persistent memory database that survives across tasks, reachable through
the `mempal` CLI (already installed, on PATH). Run it with your normal shell tool — there is no
MCP server, and you need exactly two commands.

## 1. Search before you start

```bash
mempal search "<key terms of the task>" --json --top-k 5
```

Run it once, at the start, with the task's key terms (framework, API, error message, symptom).
Empty results are a normal outcome — continue with the task and do not investigate the memory
database. Treat any hit as evidence, not authority: verify it against the current repository
before acting on it.

## 2. Record before you finish

If the work produced a durable engineering decision or a reusable debugging lesson, write a short
markdown note and ingest it:

```bash
cat > "$HOME/.mempal-notes/<short-slug>.md" <<'EOF'
# <one-line title>

<what was decided or learned, and why — 2-5 sentences, enough for a future task to act on it>
EOF
mempal ingest "$HOME/.mempal-notes" --wing memory-evals
```

Only `mempal search` and `mempal ingest` are needed. Do not call other mempal subcommands
(`status`, `projects`, `brief`, `phase3`, `knowledge*`, `cowork-*`): they print format
specifications and protocol dumps that cost context and tell you nothing about this task.

## What not to store

Never store benchmark answers, accepted proposal numbers, hidden-test guesses, raw transcripts,
or task-specific output that would reveal the answer if the same task is run again. Record the
reusable *why*, not the answer. If nothing reusable was decided, do not invent a note.

text: I’ve got the current implementation. There’s no existing `app/` tree yet, and `AppProvider` is a client-side context provider, so I need a root client wrapper in the new layout while keeping pages themselves server-first where possible.

I’m at the edit stage now. The migration plan is:

1. Create an `app/` tree with `layout.tsx`, `page.tsx`, `blog/page.tsx`, `blog/[id]/page.tsx`, `not-found.tsx`, and `global-error.tsx`.
2. Replace Pages Router data APIs with App Router patterns:
   - `getServerSideProps` -> async server component with `headers()` and uncached fetches.
   - `getStaticProps` -> server components plus `revalidate`.
   - `getStaticPaths` -> `generateStaticParams`.
   - `next/head` -> `metadata` / `generateMetadata`.
   - `pages/api/*` -> `app/api/*/route.ts` using `NextResponse`.
3. Add types for posts, comments, route params, provider context, and API payloads.
4. Remove the `pages/` directory entirely and run a build to catch App Router-specific issues.
I’m replacing the legacy router surface now. The new files will be typed TypeScript/TSX, and I’m also switching the document-level font setup to `next/font` so `_document` can go away cleanly.
The typed App Router files are in place. I’m cleaning the remaining legacy router files now, then I’ll run a build to catch anything specific to Next 16’s App Router typing and route semantics.

# Next 16 App Router migration expectations

When migrating from the Pages Router to the App Router in Next 16, route params and request helpers should be typed against the newer async model: dynamic route "'`params` are commonly handled as promises in app pages and route handlers, and `headers()` is async in server components. Replacing `getServerSideProps`, `getStaticProps`, and `getStaticPaths` with server components, `generateStaticParams`, `generateMetadata`, and route handlers avoids build-time incompatibilities and aligns the app with current routing APIs.
EOF

text:
Migrated the app fully to the App Router and removed the `pages/` tree entirely.

The new route surface is in [app/layout.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/layout.tsx), [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx), [app/blog/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/blog/page.tsx), [app/blog/[id]/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/blog/[id]/page.tsx), [app/not-found.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/not-found.tsx), [app/global-error.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/global-error.tsx), [app/api/posts/route.ts](/home/user/workspace/app/api/posts/route.ts), and [app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts](/home/user/workspace/app/api/posts/%5Bid%5D/route.ts). I replaced `getServerSideProps` with an async server component using `headers()`, replaced `getStaticProps`/`getStaticPaths` with `revalidate` and `generateStaticParams`, moved `next/head` usage to `metadata`/`generateMetadata`, and converted API routes to typed route handlers with `NextResponse`.

I also added types and shared fetch/validation helpers in [lib/posts.ts](/home/user/workspace/lib/posts.ts), converted the provider to typed client code in [components/AppProvider.tsx](/home/user/workspace/components/AppProvider.tsx), added a typed client back button in [components/BackButton.tsx](/home/user/workspace/components/BackButton.tsx), and updated [styles/globals.css](/home/user/workspace/styles/globals.css) for link-buttons and layout styling. `next build` passed, and Next generated [next-env.d.ts](/home/user/workspace/next-env.d.ts) plus updated [tsconfig.json](/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json) include entries during the successful build.
